did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN) with two branches (2D CNN for spatial features and 1D CNN for spectral features) trained via supervised learning to perform pixel-wise classification of hyperspectral images for detecting lateral tumor margins of high-risk basal cell carcinomas.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'ORIGINAL REPORT' and 'pilot study' that recruited 30 patients, performed hyperspectral imaging and Mohs micrographic surgery, and evaluated the feasibility of a convolutional neural network for tumor demarcation. It presents original experimental data, methods, and results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hyperspectral Imaging for Lateral Tumour Demarcation of High-risk Basal Cell Carcinomas during Mohs Micrographic Surgery
brief_summary: This pilot study evaluated the feasibility of using hyperspectral imaging combined with a two-branch convolutional neural network to detect lateral tumor margins of high-risk facial basal cell carcinomas prior to Mohs micrographic surgery. Thirty patients with primary facial high-risk BCCs were recruited from Sahlgrenska University Hospital in Sweden, and hyperspectral images were acquired preoperatively. The AI model, which utilized both 2D spatial and 1D spectral features, was trained using supervised learning with histopathologically verified borders as ground truth. Evaluation employed two validation strategies: a vertical split approach and leave-one-out cross-validation. The vertical split method achieved a pixel-wise accuracy of 0.76, sensitivity of 0.75, specificity of 0.78, and an AUC of 0.84, demonstrating the potential of hyperspectral imaging for tumor demarcation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused exclusively on high-risk basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) located on the face. The cohort consisted of primary facial high-risk BCCs planned for Mohs micrographic surgery. Histopathological subtypes mentioned as part of the high-risk definition include infiltrative, micronodular, morpheaform (sclerotic), basosquamous, metatypical, and perineural/perivascular growth patterns. One recurrent BCC was excluded from the final analysis.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of each pixel as tumor (malignant) or non-tumor (healthy) to detect lateral tumor margins.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states a 'pixel-wise classification approach' was used, treating each pixel as an independent sample. The model output is a 'binary classification indicating the malignancy of the central pixel'. The task involves distinguishing between histopathologically verified tumor borders and healthy skin using hyperspectral imaging data processed by a two-branch CNN. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Sahlgrenska University Hospital Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective cohort of patients with primary facial high-risk basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) recruited from the Department of Dermatology, Sahlgrenska University Hospital in Gothenburg, Sweden, between January 2022 and January 2023. Hyperspectral images and RGB images were acquired preoperatively using the Cutica prototype hyperspectral imaging system.

use.training: Used for training the convolutional neural network (CNN) via two strategies: 1) Left halves of lesion images were used for training and validation in the vertical split approach; 2) All remaining images were used for training in the leave-one-out cross-validation strategy.
use.validation: Used for validation: 1) Left halves of lesion images were used for validation in the vertical split approach; 2) No separate validation set was used in leave-one-out cross-validation (model trained on all but one lesion).
use.testing: Used for testing: 1) Right halves of lesion images were reserved for testing in the vertical split approach; 2) Each lesion image was excluded in turn and used as the test case in the leave-one-out cross-validation strategy.
use.other: Histopathologically verified borders from Mohs micrographic surgery served as ground truth for annotations.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance by using the left half of lesion images for training/validation and the right half for testing, ensuring no pixel-level information overlap.
To provide a conservative baseline estimate of model performance when generalizing to completely unseen lesions by excluding each lesion image in turn as the test case.
To quantify overall discrimination ability of the model by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
Used as the loss function during the supervised learning training process.
Used for optimizing the model's hyperparameters during training.
Employed to prevent overfitting during the training process.

statistical_methods_used.method: Data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flips and random rotations)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Applied during training to approximate the scenario of having a larger dataset and improve model robustness.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC) and confidence intervals derived from cross-validation, but no statistical hypothesis tests were conducted.

vertical_split_approach.description: Left half of lesion images used for training/validation, right half reserved for testing.
vertical_split_approach.notes: This approach reflects the performance of a model trained on a more diverse dataset covering the full variability of lesions.

leave_one_out_cross_validation.description: Each lesion image excluded in turn as the test case; model trained on all remaining images. Provides a conservative baseline estimate for generalizing to completely unseen lesions.
leave_one_out_cross_validation.notes: These results represent a conservative estimate of model performance when applied to entirely unseen lesions.

architecture_used.pretraining: The 2D CNN branch (EfficientNet) was pretrained on ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: 25x25 pixel spatial region centered on each pixel of interest; 120 spectral wavebands (460–830 nm).

architecture_details.branch_1: 2D CNN (EfficientNet) for spatial feature extraction from 3 selected spectral bands (487, 604, and 795 nm).
architecture_details.branch_2: 1D CNN for spectral feature extraction from the mean spectrum of the selected lesion half (normalized by subtracting mean and dividing by standard deviation).
architecture_details.fusion: Outputs of the two branches were concatenated into a single feature vector fed into a classification network.

training_details.regularization: Early stopping to prevent overfitting; Data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flips and random rotations).

classification_or_output_head.output: Malignancy of the central pixel (tumor vs. non-tumor)

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation of hyperspectral images into overlapping 25x25 pixel subcubes centered on each spatial pixel of interest
Selection of 3 specific spectral bands (487, 604, and 795 nm) for the 2D CNN spatial feature extraction branch
Normalization of the mean spectrum for the 1D CNN branch by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation
Data augmentation during training including horizontal and vertical flips and random rotations
Vertical splitting of lesion images into left (training/validation) and right (testing) halves to ensure no pixel-level information overlap
Annotation of tumor borders based on histopathologically verified borders from Mohs micrographic surgery

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to approximate the scenario of having a larger dataset and improve model robustness.
Applied during training to approximate the scenario of having a larger dataset and improve model robustness.

conclusions: Hyperspectral imaging combined with a two-branch convolutional neural network is feasible for preoperatively delineating the lateral margins of high-risk facial basal cell carcinomas.
The method demonstrated relatively high sensitivity (0.75) and specificity (0.78) in detecting subclinical lateral tumor extension when evaluated using a vertical split approach.
The model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.84 in the vertical split validation strategy.
Leave-one-out cross-validation, which provides a conservative estimate for generalization to unseen lesions, yielded lower performance (accuracy 0.70, sensitivity 0.52, specificity 0.80, AUC 0.66).
The study results serve as a basis for future development and further validation with larger datasets.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a small sample size (30 patients), and no sample size calculation was performed.
The dataset is geographically and demographically biased, recruited exclusively from Sahlgrenska University Hospital in Sweden (fair-skinned population).
External validation is lacking; the study explicitly states that results serve as a basis for future validation with larger datasets.

The vertical split validation approach (left half training, right half testing) introduces a risk of data leakage or optimistic bias because pixels from the same lesion are used for both training and testing, despite the claim that it approximates a larger dataset.

Leave-one-out cross-validation, which provides a more conservative estimate for generalization to unseen lesions, showed significantly lower performance (AUC 0.66) compared to the vertical split approach (AUC 0.84), indicating potential overfitting or poor generalizability.
Exclusion criteria limited the population to primary facial high-risk BCCs on regular surfaces, excluding lesions on irregular surfaces like ears or eyelids.
One recurrent BCC and 5 images were excluded due to poor quality, which may introduce selection bias.
The ground truth relies on histopathologically verified borders from Mohs micrographic surgery, which is the gold standard but subject to inter-observer variability in margin interpretation.
